resume:
GREGORY HASTINGS, MHA, FACHE
Director of Hospital Operations | Acute-Care & Ambulatory Service Lines

PROFESSIONAL SUMMARY
Healthcare operations executive with more than seventeen years of progressive leadership experience across acute-care hospitals, ambulatory networks, and integrated delivery systems. Track record of leading multidisciplinary teams through service-line growth, throughput improvement, regulatory readiness, and large-scale clinical transformation. Experienced in partnering with physician leadership, nursing, finance, and quality to align operational performance with the organization's strategic and financial objectives. Comfortable operating in highly regulated, unionized, and capacity-constrained environments. Known for building durable operating cadences, developing front-line and mid-level leaders, and representing operations to executive leadership and the board.

PROFESSIONAL EXPERIENCE

Meridian Health System — Director of Hospital Operations (2019 – Present)
Meridian is a 540-bed tertiary academic medical center with an affiliated ambulatory network of fourteen clinics and an annual operating budget in the hundreds of millions. Reporting to the Chief Operating Officer, I hold operational accountability for several inpatient and procedural service lines, including the Emergency Department, perioperative services, and patient throughput/capacity management. I lead a team of department managers, supervisors, and operational analysts, and I sit on the hospital's capacity, quality, and capital-prioritization committees.
- Provide day-to-day operational leadership for the Emergency Department, perioperative services, and central patient-flow functions, partnering closely with physician medical directors and nursing leadership.
- Co-chair the hospital's patient-throughput steering committee, which oversees length-of-stay, boarding, discharge-planning, and capacity-escalation initiatives across the enterprise.
- Led the operational workstream for a multi-year electronic health record optimization, coordinating across clinical informatics, revenue cycle, and frontline departments to stabilize workflows after go-live.
- Partner with the finance team on the annual operating budget and on monthly operating reviews for the service lines under my purview, including labor productivity, premium-pay management, and supply utilization.
- Serve as an operational lead in Joint Commission survey readiness and ongoing regulatory-compliance activities, including environment-of-care rounding and corrective-action planning.
- Sponsor a portfolio of continuous-improvement initiatives using Lean and A3 methodology, mentoring department leaders through structured problem-solving and daily management.
- Represent hospital operations in labor-management discussions with represented nursing and support staff, and participate in workforce-planning and contingency-staffing strategy.
- Developed and promoted multiple front-line supervisors into manager roles through a structured leadership-development and succession-planning approach.

St. Aldwyn Regional Medical Center — Associate Director, Patient Care Operations (2014 – 2019)
St. Aldwyn is a 280-bed community hospital serving a mixed urban and suburban population. I was responsible for operations across several medical-surgical and telemetry units, the bed-management function, and patient-transport and environmental-services coordination as it related to throughput.
- Directed daily operations for medical-surgical and telemetry nursing units in partnership with nurse managers and the Chief Nursing Officer.
- Owned the hospital's bed-management and patient-placement function, including the bed-huddle cadence and after-hours capacity escalation.
- Led a cross-functional patient-flow improvement program intended to reduce emergency-department boarding and improve the timeliness of inpatient discharges.
- Coordinated the operational response during seasonal surge periods, working with clinical leadership to flex capacity, staffing, and ancillary support.
- Partnered with case management and social work to strengthen discharge-planning processes and reduce avoidable delays in care transitions.
- Participated in the hospital's quality and patient-safety committee, supporting event review and the implementation of corrective actions.
- Managed operating and labor budgets for the assigned units and supported variance analysis with the finance department.

Northshore Ambulatory Partners — Practice Operations Manager (2010 – 2014)
Northshore operated a network of multispecialty outpatient clinics. I managed operations for a cluster of primary-care and specialty practices, with responsibility for access, scheduling, front-office workflow, and patient experience.
- Oversaw daily clinic operations for a multispecialty cluster, including front-office staff, medical assistants, and practice supervisors.
- Led initiatives to improve patient access and reduce appointment backlogs, partnering with providers on template and schedule design.
- Implemented standardized front-office and registration workflows across multiple sites to reduce variation and improve the patient and staff experience.
- Supported the operational onboarding of newly recruited physicians and advanced-practice providers.
- Managed practice-level budgets, staffing models, and vendor relationships for the assigned sites.

Starting Roles — Operations Coordinator / Analyst (2007 – 2010)
Began my healthcare career in operational-analyst and coordinator roles supporting hospital throughput reporting, staffing analysis, and process-improvement projects. Built the analytical and operational foundation that informed my progression into management and director-level roles.

EDUCATION & CREDENTIALS
- Master of Health Administration (MHA)
- Bachelor of Science, Health Sciences
- Fellow of the American College of Healthcare Executives (FACHE)
- Lean / continuous-improvement training (A3 problem-solving, daily management)

SELECTED COMPETENCIES
Service-line operations; patient throughput and capacity management; emergency-department and perioperative operations; Joint Commission and regulatory readiness; labor productivity and premium-pay management; Lean process improvement; physician and nursing partnership; budget ownership and operating reviews; leadership development and succession planning; labor-management relations in a unionized environment.

LEADERSHIP PHILOSOPHY
I believe sustainable operational performance is built on clear daily management, strong physician and nursing partnerships, and the disciplined development of front-line leaders. I focus on creating operating cadences that make problems visible early, on aligning incentives across clinical and financial stakeholders, and on coaching managers to own their results. I aim to be the kind of operations leader who is equally credible at the bedside, in the boardroom, and on the floor during a capacity crisis.
jd: Meridian Health System is recruiting a Director of Hospital Operations to own throughput, capacity, and service-line performance across our acute-care enterprise. Reporting to the COO, you will be accountable for measurable operational outcomes — emergency-department boarding hours, inpatient length-of-stay against expected, perioperative on-time starts, and labor productivity against budget — and for defending those results to executive leadership and the board. You will lead department managers and analysts, co-chair the patient-throughput steering committee, and drive Lean improvement across clinical and operational workflows. We are specifically looking for a leader who can attribute a result to the specific interventions they personally drove, quantify the impact, and explain the tradeoffs they made under capacity and labor constraints. Requirements: 8+ years of progressive healthcare-operations leadership, prior director-level accountability for throughput or service-line P&L, experience with Joint Commission readiness, and demonstrated, quantified improvement in patient flow. MHA/MBA and FACHE preferred; experience in an academic or unionized setting strongly preferred.

history.q: You co-chair the patient-throughput steering committee at Meridian. Tell me about a specific throughput problem you took on and what you did.
Let's get concrete — pick the ED boarding issue. What was the boarding time when you started, and what specifically did you change?
Who owned each of those changes? What did you personally do versus what the committee or nursing did?
And the outcome — give me the before-and-after numbers on boarding hours or length-of-stay, and over what period.

candidate_last_answer: Sure. So throughput is really the heartbeat of the hospital, and what I've learned is that it's all about partnership and alignment. We brought the right stakeholders to the table, we created visibility, and we drove a culture of accountability around flow. It was a real team effort across the enterprise. We made meaningful progress on boarding and length-of-stay — leadership was pleased with the trajectory, and the board saw the improvement. I'd say the biggest thing was getting everyone rowing in the same direction and instilling that daily operational discipline. Numbers move when the culture moves, and that's what we focused on.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: We made meaningful progress on boarding and length-of-stay

expected-yield: hard before-and-after figures on ED boarding hours and length-of-stay over a stated time window, plus the single intervention the candidate personally drove that moved them, replacing the culture narrative with defensible numbers
